What is the difference between git add and git commit?
expected: git add stages changes in the working directory for the next commit. It moves changes to the staging area (index). git commit records the staged snapshot to the repository history, creating a new commit with a message. You must add before you commit.

Rename the current branch to new-name
What is the difference between merge and rebase?
expected: Merge creates a new merge commit that combines two branches, preserving the full branch history. Rebase replays commits from one branch onto another, creating a linear history. Merge is safer for shared branches; rebase creates a cleaner history but rewrites commits.

Show a graph of the branch history
What does git reset do and what are the differences between soft, mixed, and hard?
expected: git reset moves the HEAD pointer to a specified commit. --soft keeps changes staged, --mixed (default) unstages changes but keeps them in the working directory, and --hard discards all changes completely. Use soft to recommit, mixed to restage selectively, and hard to throw away work.

Push a new local branch to remote and set up tracking
How do I update my fork with the latest changes from the original repository?
expected: First add the original repo as an upstream remote with git remote add upstream <url>. Then fetch the upstream changes with git fetch upstream. Finally merge or rebase onto your branch with git merge upstream/main or git rebase upstream/main, and push to your fork with git push origin main.

How do I resolve a merge conflict?
expected: Open the conflicted files and look for conflict markers (<<<<<<< HEAD, =======, >>>>>>> branch). Edit the files to resolve the conflicts by choosing the correct code. Then stage the resolved files with git add and complete the merge with git commit.

query: I'm in a detached HEAD state. How do I get back to my branch?
My push was rejected because the remote has changes I don't have. What do I do?
expected: Pull the remote changes first with git pull --rebase (or git pull) to integrate the remote commits with your local ones. Resolve any conflicts if they arise, then push again with git push. Alternatively use git fetch followed by git rebase origin/main and then git push.
